---
title: "Project overview"
subtitle: "An example thesis repository that renders as a GitHub Pages site"
bibliography: references.bib
---

::: {.hero-banner}
## Example thesis repository

This example models a thesis-style workflow in **Quarto**: a project overview at the top, numbered analysis chapters in a dedicated folder, reusable functions in `R/`, raw data in `data/`, and polished outputs in `figs/`.

It is designed for teaching students how to organize a research repository that is readable, reproducible, and suitable for public presentation.
:::

::: {.muted-note}
Example analytical theme: *Bombus crotchii* distribution, phenology, and spatial ecology.
:::

## Overview

This repository is organized like a thesis or dissertation workflow. The aim is to make the project legible to three audiences at once: the author, the committee, and future collaborators.

The example is framed around *Bombus crotchii* field observations and related phenology and spatial analyses. The reusable R functions in `R/analysis_functions.R` support abundance models, habitat comparisons, temporal analyses, spatial autocorrelation, distribution modeling, visualization, and validation. These are the sorts of tools a student might gradually build across a thesis project.

## Why organize a thesis repository this way?

A good thesis repository should do three things:

1. Separate raw inputs from interpretation.
2. Show the order of analytical decisions.
3. Make it easy to rebuild tables, figures, and results.

This structure does that by keeping the project overview at the top level, placing analysis chapters in a numbered folder, storing data in one place, and saving generated figures in a dedicated output directory.

## Repository structure

```text
index.qmd          <- Project overview and data description
analysis/
  01-clean.qmd     <- Data cleaning and decisions
  02-explore.qmd   <- Exploratory analysis
  03-model.qmd     <- Statistical modeling
  04-figures.qmd   <- Publication figures

data/              <- Raw data (or link to repository)
figs/              <- Saved figure outputs
R/
  analysis_functions.R <- Reusable analysis functions
```

## Data description

The project assumes three core data objects that would usually be created from raw files in `data/`:

- `surveys_df` for distribution and abundance analyses,
- `phenology_df` for flowering and bee-visit timing,
- `spatial_df` for site-level spatial analyses.

In a real thesis repository, `data/` would contain either raw CSV files, metadata files describing field collection, or a short README linking to a protected or external source.

## Analysis roadmap

The pages in `analysis/` are written in the order a reader would expect:

- `01-clean.qmd`: import data, standardize names, and record decisions,
- `02-explore.qmd`: summarize variables and inspect patterns,
- `03-model.qmd`: fit the main statistical models,
- `04-figures.qmd`: save polished figures for the thesis or manuscript.

## Reproducibility notes

This example is set up as a Quarto website so it can also serve as a GitHub Pages site. That is useful in class because students can see how one repository can support active analysis, transparent methods, and a public-facing project website.

## A few useful references

This scaffold fits naturally with familiar tools in modern R-based workflows, including Quarto, the tidyverse, and flexible modeling frameworks such as GAMs [@wickham2017r; @wood2011fast; @borcard2018numerical].
